core.title: RESILIAGE Atlas
core.tagline: WebGIS-based MultiAtlas for exploring spatial and non-spatial resilience, risk, and cultural-natural heritage data.
core.teamContext: Politecnico di Torino RESILIAGE team member; owned end-to-end implementation, deployment, data ingestion coordination, and database operations from partner-provided initial design direction

story.summary: A full-stack, cloud-native Atlas designed for an 18-partner EU Horizon Europe consortium to explore community-environment relationships through cultural, environmental, agricultural, demographic, heritage, DRR, crowdsourced, and simulation-based data.
story.problem: Stakeholders needed one geographic workspace for interpreting resilience across many evidence types, from cultural-natural assets and historical routes to risk areas, local living heritage, survey testimonies, crowdsourced media, and 3D simulations.
story.solution: A flexible WebGIS platform around structured Atlas content, synchronized exploration flows, and a dual-format mapping pipeline: PMTiles for lightweight vector overlays and an async FastAPI tile proxy for Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs). I coupled this with state-synchronized legends, an event-driven onboarding guide, and secure AWS S3 asset delivery.
story.outcome: A multidimensional resilience material into a navigable WebGIS workspace where users can explore heritage, environment, risk, community narratives, and simulations through a shared geographic lens.
highlights: Architected a dynamic raster tile proxy using FastAPI and TiTiler to stream, style, and project Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs) hosted on S3 on-the-fly.
Engineered client-side vector styling with MapLibre GL by generating dynamic 'match' expressions compiled directly from database-configured symbology configurations.
Developed a state-aware interactive tour using React Joyride that monitors active UI states (sidebar toggles, region selection, layer loads) to auto-advance steps.
Created a unified layer-management system with customized legends supporting continuous color-ramps, discrete category cards, and cyclic variables (e.g. aspect angle).
Secured private project assets (high-res cartography maps and pilot site simulation videos) using AWS S3 short-lived presigned URLs generated dynamically on-demand.
lessons: Building high-performance geospatial web tools requires tile-based delivery with formats like COGs and PMTiles, so the client only requests the data needed for the current viewport.

Highly complex scientific dashboards require active progressive disclosure; connecting tutorial events directly to application state ensures users are onboarded naturally without getting overwhelmed by the map workspace.
nextSteps: Implement a side-by-side comparison slider overlay to allow stakeholders to swipe between historical georeferenced cartography and modern satellite layers.
Develop custom client-side drawing tools to let field coordinators sketch hazard annotations and export them directly as GeoJSON features to MongoDB.

core.title: Kartino
core.tagline: AI-powered Telegram flashcards rebuilt into a production-ready learning system.

story.summary: An AI-powered Telegram flashcard product rebuilt from a prototype into a modular and production-ready learning system.
story.problem: The original idea needed to move beyond a rough prototype into something fast, maintainable, observable, and reliable enough to behave like a real product.
story.solution: I reworked the system around a more modular backend, improved core learning flows, and added testing and observability so the product could scale with more confidence.
story.outcome: Kartino became a stronger product technically and operationally, with large performance gains in the most important user flows.
highlights: Migrated from workflow prototype to modular production backend
Improved flashcard creation, lookup, and rating performance
Added stronger reliability, observability, and test coverage
lessons: Good AI products are not just about the model feature; they depend on system quality, speed, trust, and iteration loops.

core.title: RECORE
core.tagline: A resilience platform growing into its full case study.
core.teamContext: Connected platform work within the wider resilience ecosystem

story.summary: A companion platform inside the resilience space, prepared as a first-class project even while its case study is still evolving.
story.problem: Some projects are important enough to represent early, even before every screenshot and written detail is finalized.
story.solution: This entry is structured so the project can appear confidently in the portfolio now and expand naturally as its story becomes sharper.
story.outcome: The portfolio stays future-friendly and honest about work that is active, real, and still taking shape.
highlights: Prepared as a first-class project slot instead of a temporary placeholder
Leaves room for future screenshots, metrics, and technical writing
lessons: A portfolio can feel more alive when some work is shown as active and evolving instead of pretending everything is already complete.
nextSteps: Write the challenge, architecture, and impact story in more detail

core.title: Replication Toolbox
core.tagline: A future research and tooling project slot.

story.summary: A reserved slot for an upcoming project so the portfolio grows like an active workshop instead of a closed archive.
story.problem: A static portfolio can feel finished even when real work is still on the way.
story.solution: This entry reserves narrative and visual space for a serious next build without forcing fake detail too early.
story.outcome: The portfolio can signal momentum and direction while remaining honest about what is still ahead.
highlights: Signals future work without pretending it already exists
nextSteps: Replace placeholder content once the project direction is confirmed

core.title: Smart Plant Care
core.tagline: An IoT monitoring system with sensors, automation, and Telegram interaction.
core.teamContext: Project connecting hardware, interfaces, automation, and messaging

story.summary: An IoT monitoring system combining sensors, actuators, Telegram interaction, and LLM-powered reporting.
story.problem: The system had to connect physical devices, remote control, and useful monitoring in a way that stayed practical and understandable.
story.solution: I built a project spanning device communication, dashboards, automation flows, and remote interaction through Telegram.
story.outcome: The project proved range beyond standard web work and tied together software, hardware, messaging, and operations into one usable system.
highlights: Built a monitoring interface for five sensor and actuator types
Enabled real-time remote interaction via Telegram
Improved efficiency with Docker-managed control logic
lessons: Projects that bridge software and hardware tell a richer story about systems thinking and adaptability.
